items.q: What is the 'habitable zone' around a star?
items.correct: The region where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.
items.random_wrong: Where humans could survive without spacesuits.
items.why: Students assume habitable zone = inhabited or human-suitable. It's a NECESSARY but not sufficient condition for Earth-like life - based purely on temperature for liquid water; doesn't guarantee atmosphere or life.

items.q: What is the principle behind stellar parallax?
items.correct: A nearby star appears to shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: Stars actually move position throughout the year.
items.why: Students think stars literally move. Parallax is an APPARENT shift due to our changing viewpoint - same reason your finger seems to jump when you alternate eyes. The closer the star, the bigger the shift.

items.q: Why was Pluto reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006?
items.correct: It does not clear its orbital neighbourhood of similar-sized bodies.
items.misconception: It is too small to be a planet.
items.why: Students remember size as the reason. The IAU defined three criteria for planets - Pluto fails the third (orbital dominance), as it shares its region with other Kuiper Belt objects of similar size.

items.q: In a solar eclipse, what blocks the Sun?
items.why: Correct answer: The Moon. “Earth” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In a lunar eclipse, what blocks the Sun?
items.why: Correct answer: Earth. “The Moon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bodies need to line up for an eclipse (Sun, Earth, Moon)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
